attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Gynaecology clinic following an abnormal cervical screening test. The test showed high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion, and subsequent colposcopy with biopsy confirmed cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3 (CIN 3). She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She is nulliparous but hopes to have children in the future.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Colposcopy reveals an acetowhite lesion extending into the endocervical canal with abnormal vessels.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most significant long-term risk of Large Loop Excision of the Transformation Zone (LLETZ) in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient requires LLETZ procedure for treatment of CIN 3. While generally safe and effective, LLETZ carries both immediate and long-term risks that need to be discussed with the patient, particularly given her desire for future pregnancy.

Cervical incompetence is the most significant long-term risk of LLETZ, particularly relevant to this nulliparous patient. The risk increases with the depth of tissue excised and multiple procedures. Studies have shown an increased risk of preterm delivery and premature rupture of membranes in subsequent pregnancies, particularly if the excision depth exceeds 10mm. Risk is often quoted as 1/20 or 5% chance of this occurring post LLETZ.

Cervical stenosis can occur as a complication of LLETZ but is relatively uncommon, occurring in approximately 1-2% of cases. When it does occur, it may cause menstrual problems or difficulty with future cervical screening.

Chronic pelvic pain is not a recognised complication of LLETZ procedure. While some patients may experience temporary discomfort during recovery, long-term pain is not typically associated with this procedure.

Recurrent infection is not a common long-term complication of LLETZ. While there is a small risk of infection in the immediate post-operative period, this can usually be effectively treated with antibiotics.

Sexual dysfunction is not a recognised long-term complication of LLETZ. Most patients can resume normal sexual activity after 4-6 weeks when healing is complete.

The immediate risks of LLETZ include bleeding (5-10%), infection (2-3%), and post-operative pain. These complications are generally minor and self-limiting. The procedure is typically performed under local anaesthetic as a day case, with a success rate of over 90% for treating CIN.
learningPoints: Large Loop Excision of the Transformation Zone (LLETZ) is the preferred treatment for high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN 3).
The most significant long-term risk of LLETZ is cervical incompetence in future pregnancies.
Immediate complications of LLETZ include bleeding, infection, and cervical stenosis.
references: Cancer Council Australia. Treatment of HSIL [Internet]. Sydney: Cancer Council Australia; 2023 [cited 2024 Nov 23]. Available from: https://www.cancer.org.au/clinical-guidelines/cervical-cancer/cervical-cancer-screening/management-histologically-confirmed-high-grade-squamous-abnormalities/treatment-of-hsil
